speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Mr. Ellis Lewin, tape number seven. Mr. Lewin, you said there was something you wanted to add.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the-- in the late '70s, I was contacted by the Netherland government to try to identify one of the kapos that was captured in-- in-- in their country. And my name was on one-- my name was on the list of the survivors that were part of that camp, part of that scenario. And the government-- their judicial-- they-- they rule there by judges-- asked me if I could meet with them and try to identify a kapo that was-- I identified him as Chocalty He was a-- a brutal German criminal, who was in charge of-- of our-- our barracks at the time in our camp. And he was quite a brutal fellow, who was-- who took pleasure in torturing the-- the people in the camp on a very special basis. His-- his forte was to take us out in the morning in the bitter cold-- and it gets like 30, 40 below zero in-- in-- in-- in the camp-- and leave us out there for two or three hours until people just dropped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the camp called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Regensburg. It was outside of Regensburg. I don't recall the very-- the name of the camp, but I do recall this very vividly. And he-- that was this pleasure. And he would just-- would ask one of the German soldiers to line up a pistol in front of the first guy in the front line. Since they always were made-- they were having fun about Jews having big noses. He would take a-- a-- he would ask a German guard to come in there with a pistol and just aim down the first nose and fire right down the line of the first row. Well of course, it's human instinct to just draw back automatically. But that was part of the fun that Mr. Chocalty and his-- and his guards were having.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they captured him. And I was asked to a-- to a very unique way, a system, to try to identify this man. And you never forget the eyes of-- of a person like that. They can change physically, but they never change in their-- that look.

speaker: interviewee
text: And none of us had much of a problem to identify him, and consequently, he was convicted. Tried-- tried and convicted and is now serving a life sentence in the-- in the Netherlands. So I thought that would be worthwhile for some satisfaction, some conclusion of-- of a tragic situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us the process that you went through in giving your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's very-- it's very ingenious and very, very tedious. They do not-- they asked me to-- they took me in a room and-- and placed about 15 to 20 photographs of various people in different poses and hairdos and-- and- sizes. And we would then identify-- we would get down-- we would identify [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] similar to maybe five out of 20. Then we'd get down to three. And then we get down to two, and then that still wasn't sufficient. They would then bring another series of photographs. And, again in a very, very unique way, we would have to bring those down to a more credible basis. And that's how we-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you mean by, in a unique way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- I thought it was an ingenious way for them not to make a-- a mistake and an error and-- and convict the wrong person. And what I understand is this-- this group of judges traveled throughout the world, the entire world. They went to South Africa. They went to South America. They did extensive interviews in-- in the United States, Germany, anywhere that they found the survivors that were part of that camp, and this kapo was part of this-- at the time of-- of-- of his-- his tenure, so speak, they interviewed, and they used that same process of identification. So consequently, they left nothing to the imagination, and they positively identified this man. And that's good. Thank the good Lord that we were able to, because he was one of the main players as-- as a very bad person in-- in-- inside the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any attorney for the defense? Was there any cross-examination of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, this was strictly the prosecution side. I was not asked to go, because they-- it was such an open and closed case almost. Some you can't-- and he, apparently at the end, admitted to it, what I understand. But no, I never went to the trial. But we-- we were asked to-- to-- we were representing the-- the prosecution part-- the-- the-- the identification process first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did this take place, this investigation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Florida. They had-- they had-- they asked me to come down and that was a point of their interview for this part of the country. And they were in New York previously, and they were in California, and then for this part of the country, they-- they were doing interviews in a Holiday Inn in Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days. Two days. They had a-- a lot of survivors there, which they conducted the investigation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear the results of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was-- he was-- he was identified, convicted, and tried, and was given, what I understand, a life sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else to add to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand that this process is coming to an end, and I-- I don't like to see that-- I would like to see that these people are pursued for-- for eternity, if that's-- if there is such a thing. And I understand that some of the governments are now becoming lax in that. And I think that's a criminal-- it's as big a criminal act of governments to-- to not continue prosecuting as the whole Holocaust itself. And I-- I say that emphatically, because there's just no excuse for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's no excuse to-- not to continue the process. These people-- these people are murderers and a murderer is-- should be tried and-- and punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Before we conclude, are there any other experiences or events that come to mind that you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. [? Kelman, ?] I could sit here for-- for a month and tell you the experiences, but I-- I think the-- the-- the message that you are so kind, and the interview was to-- was to give me the opportunity to both be able to be heard, and hopefully, it will help the future generations and the scholars of-- of-- of-- of this endeavor that we-- that this was not a-- a-- something that we imagined or something that didn't happen as we hear today. And that there is people like myself, who are still alive and are able to attest to this. Which, unfortunately, on some of the other programs and some of the other Holocausts that happened in the-- in the past who are not-- did not have the-- the-- the ability and the technology to record this. And I think this is a very noble cause. And I-- and I thank the cause for giving us the opportunity to express ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo that we are now viewing is one that my stepmother, who was the heart specialist in the ghetto, was able to save or was able to somehow retain throughout these-- throughout the ghetto and the-- and liberation. The man in the very center there of this picture is what they used to call the Eldeste Jude, meaning the oldest Jew. And he was the President and the leader of the Lodz Ghetto. His name was Chaim Rumkowski. And he was like the President as we would know in the United States. And he formed the government inside the ghetto with-- with the-- the-- the police and the prisons and so on and schools and-- and hospitals. And Chaim Rumkowski ultimately was used to the utmost and, ultimately, was put to death by the Germans at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture you're viewing now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on a second. I'm going to get rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, the picture you're viewing now is part of the evacuation of the ghetto in 1944. And it shows how, as I indicated to you earlier in my interview, how we were being herded. And this is part of the-- the part of the trail or part of the process of going to the trains, the train station. The next one is the same. It is another form and another way of-- of showing the-- as you can see, they-- they packed their-- their belongings and their beddings. And again, this is a picture evacuating everybody. As well as the next picture is a continuation of the same evacuation of the ghetto. And it shows even, if you can see the stars that we had to wear-- the Star of David are both on the front and the back identifying us as Jews. And, again, you can see the-- the packs and the beddings and the belongings that we were told to bring with us. And we were being herded to the cars, into the boxcars, to be evacuated to the concentration camp. The same-- this is the same procedure in another photo. Again, these were able to be retained and saved by my stepmother, who again, was able to stay in the ghetto to the very end and was able to save these pictures, retain them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the photos you're viewing now is the actual march, the evacuation of-- again, you can see the people are being herded to the-- in the direction, again, of the station. And the wagons that some of these belongings were brought there, and now they're coming back empty. They were full with the belongings and full of-- of-- of items that people were trying to take with them, and now, as you can see, they're coming back empty. This is my certificate of release from-- from Auschwitz and Dachau given to me by the Camp Commander of the United States forces of the Third Armored Division in September of 1945. And this is part of my identity as being a-- a-- a inmate in the concentration camp, both at Dachau and Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photo was taken right after the-- the war in Germany, and when we were trying all to get together and-- and decide where we wanted to go. I am the second from the left. These are survivor fellows. They were all young, around my age. I would say the oldest there, perhaps, was about 14 or 15. I, at the time, was 12 years old. And we were just using the comradeship to-- to feel glad that we survived at that juncture. Again, this is a photo with some girls that survived and again, with some of the fellows. And some of these people went to Israel and others went with me to United States. This, again, is after the war, just before we're leaving-- just before we were leaving Germany to go on to our designated places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the photo that-- that was taken by the upon our arrival to Cleveland. The lady in the center is my foster mother and Winifred Fryer, who was our sponsor and who brought-- took us into her home. And we were her foster sons, and who had given us the opportunity to pursue our-- our musical aspirations. And she was just a wonderful, philanthropic lady. And my cousin on the right, is Jack Begalman, who is a survivor, and he and I came on that same transport over here. And both of us were placed in a home, since both of our interests were in music. And of course, that's me on the left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photo represents me receiving my citizens papers in the United States Army at the 24th Infantry Division, Six Tank Battalion. My company-- my-- the Commander of the battalion was giving me the-- the honor of my citizenship in the services during the Korean conflict. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph represents my family on the 50th-- this is-- was taken on the 50th anniversary in Cleveland at the Fairmount Temple for the survival-- of the anniversary of the 50th year. The one on the left is my son, my oldest son, Ricky, my wife Joan. I'm in the center, and my son, Dennis, my daughter, Debbie, and my grandson, Adam. The other little one was too little to-- to appear in this picture yet, but I'm going to be proud to show him on the next picture. OK, these are my two grandkids. The one on the left is Adam, and the one sitting up is Andrew. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family that-- that-- is in-- in-- let's start again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Anytime you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I want to introduce my family. My wife, Joan, on my right here, and my oldest son, Ricky, on my right. And my son, Dennis, my daughter, Debbie, my grandson, Adam, and my grandson, Andrew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody wave just for the hell of it. Does anybody want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have this to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Papa. My Papa's name is Ellis Lewin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Lodz, Poland, which is in Europe. My Papa was forced at the age that-- at eight--

speaker: interviewee
text: That we are now.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans to go to a concentration camp. He lost his mother and sister, and I know that that must have been very scary for him and this makes me sad. My great-grandfather did survive, and I am lucky to still have him in my life. After the war ended my Papa was sent to the United States, and the place he came to is [INAUDIBLE]. He married my Nana in 1956. He was three-- he has three children. My mom is his daughter, and in 1988, when I was born, I knew I had the best Papa in the world. My Papa is a very successful businessman and has come the longest way, and I am proud of him. I am so glad God saved my Papa so that I could be here today. I love you, Papa. Adam Bankhurst.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's what it was all worth.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what it all is about.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what it's all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what they have-- a wonderful grandfather and father and husband. And I thank God every day that we-- I was born here in the United States-- and that this would never, ever happen to my children. And I thank Ellis for the inner strength he gave to me from what he came from, and what he's done with this life and how good he's been to all of us. And we love him, and we thank God for him, to bring him here.
